data.title: Bebop
data.tagline: Learning at the feet of giants
data.article:
Miles Dewey Davis III arrived in New York City in September 1944, ostensibly to study at Juilliard. He had a different curriculum in mind.

Within weeks he had found Charlie Parker and Dizzy Gillespie, the twin architects of bebop — the revolutionary new jazz built on breakneck tempos, advanced harmonics, and virtuosic improvisation. Miles absorbed everything, sitting in wherever he could, recording his first sides as a sideman with Bird in 1945.

His tone was already distinct: less aggressive than Dizzy's acrobatics, more lyrical and searching. Critics noticed. By 1947 he was leading his own sessions. By 1948 he was restless.

Bebop gave Miles his foundation. He would spend the rest of his career dismantling it.
